mnemonics: D.U.B. - Discrete Uniform Binomial. Remember the main discrete distributions by thinking of a DUB step (a simple, distinct move) for counting outcomes.
P.E.N. - Poisson, Exponential, Normal. Remember the main continuous distributions by thinking of writing with a PEN on a smooth, continuous line.
keyPoints: A probability distribution describes how probabilities are distributed over the values of a random variable.
Discrete distributions (e.g., Uniform, Binomial, Poisson) model countable outcomes.
Continuous distributions (e.g., Normal, Exponential) model measurements and uncountable outcomes.
commonMistakes: Confusing the Binomial (n trials, probability p) and Geometric (number of trials until first success) distributions.
Using a discrete distribution for a continuous variable (e.g., using Binomial for a person's height).
Misapplying the 68-95-99.7 rule for a Normal distribution to distributions that are not normal or not standardized.
